feedback.text: I wholeheartedly agree with this initiative, especially the issue around dark patterns and addictive design, especially concerning microtransactions and loot boxes in video games. Concerning the dark patterns, I'd even go a step further and say that companies, where available, should be forced to support open standards. For example, instead of just outlawing dark patterns for cookie banners, companies should be forced to support an open interface that allows users to automatically set cookie preferences. That way, you wouldn't have to set your cookie preferences for every site manually, you could set them in the browser settings once and the browser will automatically tell the websites your cookie preferences. I would however also like to advocate for one additional goal to be added to this legislation: Force gaming companies to stop killing video games. What I mean by this is that I would like to see legislation included that forces video game developers to implement an end-of-life plan during game development, that allows the core gameplay experience to be preserved, once the video game publisher decides to no longer support a video game. One good example of where the a video game developer needlessly killed a video game is The Crew. The Crew was a racing game that was mainly focussed on a single-player experience, with multiplayer elements that were not part of the core gameplay experience. Ubisoft, the publisher, could have decided to simply patch out the multiplayer elements. Instead, they chose to shut down the servers. And because a server connection was always required, even though the multiplayer experience was not part of the core gameplay experience, the game is now no longer playable. Another reason to add a provision to stop killing video games is the cultural impact. People that grew up with video games may be familiar with the phrase "But can it run Crysis?". Crysis is a single-player shooter that can still be played, that, for its time, had very detailed graphics and was overly demanding on the computer hardware. The question is alluding to Crysis needing top-of-the-line computer components to run smoothly. On the other end of the spectrum we have the phrase "Can it run Doom?". Doom is an old shooter that is best known for its modest hardware requirements. People over the time have taken it as a challenge to run the game Doom on as many unlikely devices as possible, such as refrigerators, printers or old phones. What this goes to show is that old games that are still playable had and continue to have cultural impact. Nowadays however, most games will eventually be killed. Anyone young enough of with children young enough will probably have heard of Fortnite. And while I am sure some are looking forward to Fortnite being shut down for good, I think it is not an understatement to say that Fortnite today has the same impact as Crysis or Doom had years ago. The difference is that Crysis and Doom can still be played today, but as it stands now, Fortnite will be shut down as soon as the developer decides it is no longer profitable. To prevent such huge cultural impacts being lost to time in the not so distant future, I am advocating for this legislation to be amended to also call for video game developers and publishers to stop killing video games.
